title: Data Analyst Technical Round
description: Five scenario questions. Each question focuses on one practical data analysis decision.

questions.title: Monthly Revenue Query

scenario.context: You have a customers table with one row per customer and an orders table with order date, status, and amount. The business wants monthly revenue and unique purchasing customers by region.
constraints: A customer can place multiple orders in the same month.
scenario.prompt: Describe the SQL logic you would use, how you would avoid double counting, and one way you would validate the result.

questions.objective: Assess whether the candidate can reason about filtering, joins, aggregation, and query validation.

target_elements.description: Filters valid orders and identifies the order-level input grain.
Joins customers safely and calculates revenue and distinct customers by month and region.
Uses a simple reconciliation or duplicate check.

probes.trigger: The candidate counts order rows as customers.
probes.prompt: How would you count a customer who places three orders in the same month?

probes.trigger: The candidate does not consider join duplication.
probes.prompt: What would you check if revenue increases after joining customers?

hint_ladder: Filter the orders first, then join and aggregate.
Use SUM for revenue, COUNT DISTINCT for customers, and reconcile totals before and after the join.

dimensions.name: problem_framing
dimensions.description: Recognizes the requested grain, measures, and exclusions.

anchors.1: Misses the requested output or cancellation rule.
anchors.5: Clearly identifies the input and output grains and required filters.

dimensions.name: technical_depth
dimensions.description: Uses sound filtering, join, grouping, and distinct-count logic.

anchors.5: Provides precise logic and anticipates join cardinality.

dimensions.name: data_correctness_reliability
dimensions.description: Prevents double counting and validates the output.

anchors.1: Would double count revenue or customers.
anchors.3: Produces correct measures and includes a basic check.
anchors.5: Also checks unmatched keys and reconciles totals across stages.

dimensions.name: tradeoff_reasoning
dimensions.description: Explains a meaningful join or aggregation choice.

anchors.5: Concise reasoning covers both correctness and business meaning.

dimensions.name: communication
dimensions.description: Explains the query in a clear sequence.

anchors.1: The logic is difficult to follow.
anchors.5: The filter, join, aggregate, and validation steps are concise and ordered.

strong_answer_outline: Filter the orders to the requested date range and valid statuses, then derive the reporting month from order_date.
Join each order to the single matching customer, group by month and region, calculate SUM(amount), and use COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) for purchasing customers.
Compare order counts and revenue before and after the join, and check for duplicate customer rows or unmatched customer IDs.
acceptable_alternatives: An inner join or a left join is acceptable if the treatment of customers with an unknown region is stated.
Pre-aggregating orders by customer and month is acceptable when the final revenue total remains correct.
red_flags: Counts order rows as unique customers.
Joins to a non-unique customer table without checking cardinality.
Includes canceled orders or performs no validation.

questions.title: Conversion Rate Definition

scenario.context: Marketing and Product report different website conversion rates. Users can visit more than once, and a purchase may happen up to seven days after the first visit.
constraints: The company needs one definition for weekly reporting.
Test users and bots should not affect the metric.
scenario.prompt: Define a weekly conversion-rate metric, including its grain, numerator, denominator, attribution window, and one validation check.

questions.objective: Assess whether the candidate can turn an ambiguous business term into a consistent, testable metric.

target_elements.description: Defines eligible converted users and the matching eligible population.
Chooses a user or session grain and a clear conversion window.
Documents exclusions and validates that numerator records belong to the denominator.

probes.trigger: The candidate mixes visits and users in the same ratio.
probes.prompt: Would one user with five visits appear once or five times in your denominator?

probes.trigger: The candidate does not address delayed purchases.
probes.prompt: How would a purchase six days after the first visit be counted?

hint_ladder: Choose either a user-based or session-based metric and keep both sides of the ratio at that grain.
State who is eligible, what counts as conversion, and how long a conversion can occur after the starting event.

dimensions.name: problem_framing
dimensions.description: Clarifies the business question behind conversion.

anchors.1: Uses the term conversion without defining it.
anchors.3: Identifies the main event and eligible audience.
anchors.5: Connects the grain and window to the weekly business decision.

dimensions.name: technical_depth
dimensions.description: Defines a coherent numerator, denominator, grain, and attribution window.

anchors.1: Mixes incompatible grains or time windows.
anchors.5: Defines eligibility and timing precisely without overcomplicating the metric.

dimensions.name: data_correctness_reliability
dimensions.description: Keeps the ratio consistent and excludes invalid traffic.

anchors.1: The numerator is not a valid subset of the denominator.
anchors.3: Uses consistent populations and basic exclusions.
anchors.5: Also includes a clear reconciliation or monitoring check.

dimensions.name: tradeoff_reasoning
dimensions.description: Explains the effect of the selected grain or attribution window.

anchors.5: Explains how the choice changes interpretation and comparability.

dimensions.name: communication
dimensions.description: States the metric in language that teams can apply consistently.

anchors.5: The definition is concise, testable, and ready to document.

strong_answer_outline: Use a user-based weekly cohort: the denominator is unique eligible users whose first qualifying visit occurs that week, excluding bots and test users.
The numerator is those same users who complete a valid purchase within seven days of that qualifying visit, using a documented time zone and purchase-status rule.
Check that every converted user exists in the denominator, compare counts with source events, and publish the definition with its exclusions and window.
acceptable_alternatives: A session-based conversion rate is acceptable if both numerator and denominator are session-based and attribution is explicit.
A same-week or same-day window is acceptable if it matches the business use case and is applied consistently over time.
red_flags: Divides purchases by visits while describing the result as user conversion.
Changes exclusions or the denominator between reports.

questions.title: Weekly Sales Dashboard

scenario.context: Sales leaders need a weekly dashboard showing revenue versus target. Executives want a quick overview, while regional managers need to identify which regions or products are behind.
constraints: The first page should be understandable in under a minute.
The dashboard should support region and product filtering.
scenario.prompt: What would you put on the first page, which chart types would you use, and how would you prevent the dashboard from being misleading?

questions.objective: Assess whether the candidate can select focused visuals that support overview and diagnosis.

target_elements.description: Prioritizes overall performance, trend, and the main breakdown.
Uses chart types appropriate for comparisons and trends.
Adds clear definitions, targets, filters, refresh context, and honest scales.

probes.trigger: The candidate lists many charts without prioritization.
probes.prompt: What are the first three things an executive should see?

probes.trigger: The candidate does not discuss visual integrity.
probes.prompt: What chart or labeling choice could exaggerate a small performance gap?

hint_ladder: Start with current performance, change over time, and where the gap comes from.
Use a KPI, a trend line, and a ranked comparison with clear targets, dates, units, and filters.

dimensions.name: problem_framing
dimensions.description: Addresses both executive overview and manager investigation.

anchors.3: Supports the main needs of both audiences.
anchors.5: Creates a clear overview-to-detail path.

dimensions.name: technical_depth
dimensions.description: Selects appropriate visuals and interactions.

anchors.5: Uses a focused hierarchy of complementary visuals.

dimensions.name: data_correctness_reliability
dimensions.description: Shows consistent metrics, targets, dates, units, and refresh state.

anchors.1: Leaves important metric or time context ambiguous.
anchors.5: Also exposes definitions, freshness, and filter state.

dimensions.name: tradeoff_reasoning
dimensions.description: Balances speed of understanding with useful detail.

anchors.5: Explains what belongs on the first page and what should be drill-down detail.

dimensions.name: communication
dimensions.description: Presents a clear visual story with honest emphasis.

anchors.1: The dashboard concept is cluttered or misleading.
anchors.5: The visual order, labels, and annotations make the action obvious.

strong_answer_outline: Show headline revenue, target, and variance first, followed by a weekly revenue-versus-target trend.
Use a ranked bar chart or compact table for regional performance, with filters or drill-down for product detail.
Use clear units, dates, metric definitions, refresh time, consistent colors, and honest axes; keep nonessential charts off the first page.
acceptable_alternatives: Small multiples by region are acceptable when there are few regions and trend comparison is important.
A variance table with conditional formatting is acceptable when managers need exact values.
red_flags: Uses many pie charts or an overcrowded first page.
Uses truncated axes or color without labels to exaggerate differences.
Shows revenue without target, date range, or metric definition.

questions.title: Regional Active-User Drop

scenario.context: Daily active users fall 20 percent in one region on the same day a new tracking version is released. Other regions appear stable.
constraints: The team needs to know whether user behavior changed or measurement broke.
A conclusion is needed before the next daily report.
scenario.prompt: Describe the first analysis steps you would take to separate a real business decline from a tracking or data issue.

questions.objective: Assess whether the candidate can validate a metric, segment the change, and reach an evidence-based conclusion.

target_elements.description: Checks the active-user definition, completeness, timing, and baseline.
Segments by useful dimensions and compares affected and unaffected groups or sources.
States what evidence would support each explanation and how findings would be communicated.

probes.trigger: The candidate assumes the release caused the drop.
probes.prompt: What evidence would show that tracking changed rather than user behavior?

probes.trigger: The candidate only compares with the previous day.
probes.prompt: What comparison period would help control for normal weekly patterns?

hint_ladder: First confirm the metric and data completeness, then segment the drop.
Compare app versions, platforms, events, and unaffected regions against a normal day-of-week baseline.

dimensions.name: problem_framing
dimensions.description: Frames the competing business and measurement explanations.

anchors.5: States clear evidence that would distinguish them.

dimensions.name: technical_depth
dimensions.description: Uses useful baselines, segments, and source comparisons.

anchors.3: Checks relevant segments and recent trends.
anchors.5: Uses affected-versus-control comparisons to isolate the cause.

dimensions.name: data_correctness_reliability
dimensions.description: Validates metric logic and data completeness before concluding.

anchors.1: Draws a conclusion from incomplete or unverified data.
anchors.3: Checks definition, freshness, and event volume.
anchors.5: Also reconciles against an independent source or unaffected event.

dimensions.name: tradeoff_reasoning
dimensions.description: Prioritizes checks that provide fast, useful evidence.

anchors.1: Proposes broad analysis with no priority.
anchors.5: Explains a short sequence that balances speed and confidence.

dimensions.name: communication
dimensions.description: Communicates findings with appropriate confidence.

anchors.3: Explains the likely cause and evidence.
anchors.5: Also states impact, confidence, and the next validation step.

strong_answer_outline: Confirm the active-user definition, time zone, refresh completeness, and whether the drop remains against the same weekday and recent trend.
Break the change down by app version, platform, event type, and user segment; compare the affected region with stable regions and compare tracked events with an independent source if available.

If only the new version loses a key event, treat it as tracking and correct or backfill the metric; if multiple independent signals decline, investigate the business drivers and communicate the evidence and confidence.
acceptable_alternatives: A before-and-after comparison is acceptable when seasonality and other simultaneous changes are addressed.
A temporary dashboard warning is appropriate while measurement integrity is uncertain.
red_flags: Assumes the tracking release or the business is responsible without testing.
Looks only at the regional total.
Reports a conclusion before checking freshness and event completeness.

questions.title: Checkout Experiment Result

scenario.context: An A/B test shows checkout conversion is 3 percent higher in the treatment group. The sample is still small, the treatment group has more mobile users, and checkout-error rate is a guardrail metric.
constraints: The product team wants a rollout recommendation.
Both conversion impact and checkout reliability matter.
scenario.prompt: What would you check before recommending a rollout, and how would you summarize the result if the evidence is not yet conclusive?

questions.objective: Assess whether the candidate can evaluate experiment validity, uncertainty, practical impact, and guardrails.

target_elements.description: Checks randomization, sample balance, exposure, duration, and data quality.
Evaluates effect size with uncertainty rather than relying on the observed uplift alone.
Examines device imbalance and the checkout-error guardrail before making a recommendation.

probes.trigger: The candidate accepts the overall uplift without checking group comparability.
probes.prompt: Why could the extra mobile users change the observed result?

probes.trigger: The candidate recommends rollout based only on the positive point estimate.
probes.prompt: What would you report if the confidence interval includes no effect?

hint_ladder: Check whether the groups are comparable before interpreting the uplift.
Report the effect and its uncertainty, then check device segments and the error-rate guardrail.

dimensions.name: problem_framing
dimensions.description: Frames the decision around conversion, reliability, and uncertainty.

anchors.5: Clearly defines the evidence needed for a rollout decision.

dimensions.name: technical_depth
dimensions.description: Checks assignment, balance, sample, effect size, and uncertainty.

anchors.5: Connects experiment validity, effect size, and practical significance.

dimensions.name: data_correctness_reliability
dimensions.description: Validates exposure data, group comparability, and guardrails.

anchors.3: Checks the main validity and guardrail measures.
anchors.5: Also checks sample-ratio or instrumentation anomalies.

dimensions.name: tradeoff_reasoning
dimensions.description: Balances potential conversion gain with uncertainty and user risk.

anchors.1: Recommends rollout or rejection without weighing risks.
anchors.5: Makes a proportionate recommendation such as continue, stop, or limited rollout.

dimensions.name: communication
dimensions.description: Summarizes evidence without overstating certainty.

anchors.1: Presents an inconclusive result as a win.
anchors.3: States the result and uncertainty clearly.
anchors.5: Gives a concise recommendation, rationale, and next step.

strong_answer_outline: Check random assignment, sample-ratio balance, exposure logging, experiment duration, and whether the mobile imbalance is random or indicates a setup problem.
Report the absolute and relative conversion difference with a confidence interval or equivalent uncertainty measure, and compare it with a meaningful business threshold.
Review conversion by device and the checkout-error guardrail; if the result is uncertain or errors worsen, do not claim a win and recommend continuing or correcting the test before rollout.
acceptable_alternatives: Frequentist or Bayesian analysis is acceptable if uncertainty and the decision threshold are explained.
A limited rollout is acceptable when risk is controlled and monitoring and stop criteria are explicit.
red_flags: Rolls out because the point estimate is positive.
Ignores device imbalance or the checkout-error guardrail.
Uses statistical significance alone without considering effect size or business impact.
